mainText:
**The Post-Dobbs Abortion Pill Fight**

**Trump Rejects New Iranian Proposal**
Iranian negotiators have sent their official reply to the ceasefire proposal submitted by U.S. officials last week. The Iranians reportedly want negotiations to focus on ending the war, including in Lebanon, and rejected U.S. demands to dismantle their nuclear facilities and suspend uranium enrichment for 20 years. They instead offered a five-year suspension, dilution of part of their uranium stockpile, and transfer of the rest to a third country. President Donald Trump wrote on Truth Social that the proposal was "TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE!"

On Sunday, CBS News aired an interview with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who stated that the war with Iran had accomplished a "great deal," but emphasized that it was "not over." The Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed through the weekend after multiple skirmishes between U.S. and Iranian forces, along with reported Iranian drone attacks on the territories of Qatar, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates.

**April Jobs Report Beats Expectations**
A Friday report from the Department of Labor found that U.S. employers added 115,000 jobs in April, significantly higher than the 55,000 forecast by economists. The healthcare sector alone added 37,000 jobs, while transportation and warehousing companies added an additional 30,000. The average number of jobs added per month over the last three months has decreased to 48,000, down from roughly 73,000 during the November to January period. The overall unemployment rate remained unchanged from March at 4.3 percent, but the labor force shrank, indicating that more unemployed people may have given up looking for work.

April’s stronger-than-expected numbers came after a March jobs report that also exceeded expectations, showing a growth of 185,000 jobs, following a February in which the U.S. economy lost 156,000 jobs. The relative strength of the labor market has increased investor expectations that the Federal Reserve will focus more on inflation than unemployment, reducing the chances of a rate cut later this year.

**Russia and Ukraine Each Claim the Other Violated Weekend Ceasefire**
Russia and Ukraine accused each other of violating a three-day ceasefire on Sunday, which was meant to extend from Saturday to Monday to allow Russia to celebrate Victory Day. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky asserted that while Russia had refrained from large-scale attacks, it was not genuinely attempting to cease combat along the front lines. Russia accused Ukrainian forces of conducting attacks on multiple civilian targets. Trump claimed that Zelensky and Vladimir Putin had agreed to the ceasefire and a prisoner swap at his request.

On Saturday, Putin presided over the Victory Day parade in Moscow’s Red Square, featuring thousands of Russian troops and North Korean forces, though tanks and other military equipment were absent due to the threat of Ukrainian drone attacks. Putin later stated that he believed the war in Ukraine was "coming to an end" while vowing victory over Ukraine during his speech at the parade.

**Virginia’s High Court Overturns Congressional Map**
Virginia’s Supreme Court on Friday overturned last month’s referendum on redrawing the state’s congressional boundaries, which had approved a Democratic Party-authored plan favoring the party. In a 4-3 ruling, the court held that the referendum violated Virginia’s constitution, which requires proposed constitutional amendments to pass two consecutive legislative sessions, with an election in between, before being placed on the ballot. Democrats spent $65 million during the April special election that approved the map with a 52 percent "Yes" vote. Democratic statehouse leaders have appealed the ruling to the U.S. Supreme Court, requesting an emergency hearing.

**Trump Reportedly Plans to Fire FDA Commissioner**
President Donald Trump has reportedly approved a plan to fire FDA Commissioner Martin Makary, following several controversial decisions that angered various groups. Makary faced criticism for overruling FDA scientific staff who recommended approval of fruit-flavored e-cigarettes, leading to pressure from the vaping industry and a personal rebuke from Trump. Makary also faced backlash from anti-abortion activists for not delaying the approval of a generic version of mifepristone
